title: Present Tense Verbs
summary: Most Catalan verbs follow predictable conjugation patterns in the present tense.

sections.content: Catalan verbs are grouped into three conjugation classes based on their infinitive ending: -ar (most common), -er/-re, and -ir. The good news: within each group the pattern is consistent, so once you learn the pattern you can conjugate hundreds of verbs.
Unlike English, Catalan verb endings carry the subject — so the pronoun is often dropped. 'Parlo' means 'I speak' by itself; you don't need 'jo' unless you're emphasising.
-AR verbs (e.g. parlar = to speak): drop -ar, add endings: -o, -es, -a, -em, -eu, -en
-ER/-RE verbs (e.g. córrer = to run): drop -er/-re, add: -o, -s, (nothing), -em, -eu, -en
-IR verbs (e.g. dormir = to sleep): drop -ir, add: -o, -s, (nothing), -im, -iu, -en. Some -ir verbs insert -eix- in singular and 3rd plural forms.

Some very common verbs are irregular and must be memorised individually: ser (to be), estar (to be), tenir (to have), anar (to go), fer (to do/make), poder (to be able to), voler (to want). You've already seen ser and estar.

items.catalan: tinc / tens / té / tenim / teniu / tenen
items.english: I have / you have / he has...

items.catalan: vaig / vas / va / anem / aneu / van
items.english: I go / you go / he goes...

items.catalan: faig / fas / fa / fem / feu / fan
items.english: I do / you do / he does...

sections.question: 'Parlar' means 'to speak'. How do you say 'she speaks'?
sections.explanation: 3rd person singular of -ar verbs takes the ending -a: parl + a = parla.

sections.question: How do you say 'we sleep' using 'dormir'?
sections.explanation: -IR verbs use -im for 'we': dorm + im = dormim.

sections.question: In Catalan, do you need the subject pronoun 'jo' (I) to mean 'I speak'?
sections.explanation: Verb endings encode the subject in Catalan. 'Parlo' means 'I speak' on its own. 'Jo parlo' is grammatically fine but only used for emphasis.

sections.question: How do you say 'I have' using 'tenir'?
sections.explanation: 'Tenir' is irregular. The 1st person singular is 'tinc'. You've seen it in 'Tinc gana' (I'm hungry) and 'Tinc set' (I'm thirsty) from the starter deck.
